speaker: interviewer
text: G-O-L-D-B-A-R-T-E-N. This is roll number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, they had open cellars. They used to put in there potatoes-- their-- all their vegetables. What he did-- he crawls in this. The cellar was outside, separate-- I don't know if you have an idea. Later, where he kept his horses and the cattle, it was not far from the cellar. He made a hole. And over there, he put me in-- in this hole, so I could go into the cellar. And I was in the cellar. He kept me in, and on the top, he covered, I don't know with what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you go to the cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: So no, what I want to bring you out-- if somebody would tell he was smart and he saved himself, don't believe it. It's plain destiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning, they were-- the police were Ukrainian. And later, I'm not going to tell you the reason why they changed, or you want me to tell you the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Ukrainian Galicia-- Galicia was Poland. The just-- the Ukrainian in Galicia, they were the worst Ukrainian than ours. Our Ukrainians, they lived with us good. I do remember, like a child in the First World War, they came-- they came, the  cavalry Ukrainian. They called them [NON-ENGLISH]. They came from Galicia to our town, and they want to make a pogrom. This was in 1918, or '19. And at that time, everybody had guns, because it was the-- the revolution, the Russian. Our Ukrainians came out with guns, and then told them. Whatever you want, we'll go to the Jews. And we will tell them, and they will give you. If you want to make a pogrom here, you'll have to fight us. I want to just give you an example of the difference between this same Ukrainian in Galicia, and ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to us. We gave them cigarettes. They wanted-- they wanted-- they wanted for the horses, we gave them everything. I want to just show you the difference. That was before the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was during the Russian Revolution. This was. So when he-- when they started drinking, he told them I-- later on, in Poland, the Ukrainians in Galicia, they were-- they were against Poland. They didn't want to be under Poland's occupation, they called this. There was a student in Limburg, [NON-ENGLISH], they called this. They was-- under the University of Ukraine, by the name Bandera. He went to Hitler, and he told him he would see to it that all the Ukrainians should go. They should-- they should-- they should drop the Russian army and go by themselves, free, as prisoners of war. And he promised them, when he will-- when he will occupy the whole Ukraine, he would give him-- he'll make him [NON-ENGLISH] of Ukraine-- that means-- that means us-- a Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning, they were helping the Germans, the Ukrainians. The police were now telling they were Ukrainian. And when he came after the-- after the German army was not far from Moscow, they had told the Ukrainian occupiers that came to them and asked him to do it. He put them in jail. So the Ukrainian police took the guns, and they ran away, and they became partisans. In the woods, they called them Banderovcis, they called them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to mention one thing. When there was the action, they ran away from our town, many, many young Jewish people and girls into the woods. Our woods-- no German soldier had the courage to go into our woods. All of the people were killed by the Banderovcis, by the Ukrainians. They killed them all. They killed them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those were the good Ukrainians? They were good to the Jews before?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before. Now-- [SIGHS] now, after they saw that a Jew is lawless-- a Jew is worse than a dog. If a Ukrainian hit a dog, they would put them in jail. If they hit a Jew, they-- nothing. They saw what-- what we-- you see the same thing. What Hitler did-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he broke our resistance. He put-- he made us feel so-- like I started believing maybe I'm-- maybe I'm not a human being. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: If I have-- if I have no right to walk on the sidewalk, he can do with me whatever he wants. I didn't commit a crime. Why am I punished? For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have no idea the  pain. When I was beaten-- when they-- when they beat me, I didn't feel physical pain. [CRYING] I didn't feel. When I had to walk where the horses and dogs, then I felt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came and they caught me. They came-- In the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so they came over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Banderovcis, the partisans, Ukrainian. And they had   a flashlight. And I saw a revolver. And he says to me-- they called my name. He says to me, come out. So I was quiet. Come out! If not, I'm going to shoot. So I came out. And they took me. They took me. They brought me to a farm. There were three partisans, Ukrainian. Why I'm telling you this story, I am just showing you there only destiny-- only destiny. He brought me to a house. It was a single house on a farm. There were three partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put them in the room-- there were three beds. I was in one. Yeah, I had a beard, and I had everything. So-- so they shaved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me a lie. You are an intelligent man, you're going to work for us. And it was a lie. They killed all the Jews whom they caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did-- I did believe. Like I told you, a man who drowns-- he wants to save it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and over there was a young girl. I didn't even know she worked for me. Over there was a-- was a-- was a young girl. So-- so he was with the girl, and one was different. And I was in one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she asked him, what are you going to do with-- with Emil? He says, what are we going to do? We're going to kill him. And she says, oh, it's-- he was such a nice man. I worked for him, he was so nice to me. Why? She says, I owe you this. So I had nothing to lose. So do you know, they don't have, like here, toilets. You have to do it outside. So when I heard this, so I went and I told them I have a stomach ache. So he told me, so go-- go outside. So I told him it's not-- yeah, this was 1944, in January. Winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I went and  I  told him. He says, take that flashlight. And I saw a revolver. I put it into my pocket. And I decided, alive they will not take me. And I start running. And the snow was up to here. And I saw it-- so it started getting already light.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran over there to a farm in a barn. And I started digging a hole in the straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, somebody opens the door already from the barn. So I didn't have time to make a  hole I could stretch my leg. And this saved my-- saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it didn't take too long. They came in, and they asked her, did you see a Jew here? She says, she came in to take the straw. She says, no. So they took-- you know the fork on the farm, where you put to take straw? So he gave him one, and he says, stick one next to the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: It so happened I was sitting in the hole like that, not stretched. And he was standing on my head. And he sticking, sticking.

speaker: interviewer
text: If he would have hit me, I would scream. He would stick and stick. And he says, what the hell? Then-- where-- where did he disappear?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in this barn seven days, eating nothing. Just in the night, I would go out and eat the snow. My whole mouth was dry. And I decided, I'll-- I'll die anyway, not knowing where to go. And the nighttime, I went. And it so happened I came to this guy, to the Karpo. And the barns are open. I went in the barn. And I hid myself. Later when he came in the barn, I told him, I am back. And in two weeks or three, I was liberated. Now, I want to just tell you one thing. The instinct in a human being of survival is so strong-- so strong-- I did convince myself that the body creates extra energy, I don't know. I was laying 16 months in the same clothing and the same underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was hiding in the barns, not taking a bath-- laying by myself, not to talk to nobody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How-- how I am of sound mind-- [CRYING] I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't believe [CRYING] I went through, and I could-- and I could do it. I had no chance-- no chance to survive. I didn't know how long Hitler almost got the whole world. I didn't know. Just still the instinct of survival is so strong. You want to stay alive. You want to stay alive. I didn't know for different reasons. I want to stay alive to tell-- to tell-- to tell the world what they did to us. And I was liberated in 1944, in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: In February 1944? Who liberated you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Soviet Union-- the Soviet army. Soviet army. And all of the Jewish people-- all the Jewish-- I mean, all the-- we were about 10 or 15-- that's all from the whole town, who survived. Our town between 4,000 and 5,000 people-- only 10 or 15 people-- we came in one town together. Zdolbunow, what I said before. Over there, we came together.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the liberation, the Russians took you to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came-- we came by ourselves-- we came, I don't know how we hired a Ukrainian to-- with the horses. When we came to this town, all the survivors became-- oh, they were-- we were about 10 or 15 people. We came to this town. And later--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say the name of the town again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zdolbunow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-D-O-L-V-U-N-O-W. They came over there. And later on, I was working for the Soviet Union. Later because when they kept going further up, our part was liberated. So I was working for them as a head cashier in the-- the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they liberate you? Do you remember? You were hiding in the barn, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in our town, the soldiers, they-- the Russian army. And they left probably, I don't know, because he still kept me hiding. He was afraid, the Ukrainian. Just, when they came in, they took me in already in the house. When the Russian army came in, they took me the barn in the house. In the house, they took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told them that you were there? The Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Who told him whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were hiding in the barn. Who told them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back, after I ran away from the Banderovcis, I came to him. And when-- and when he came in the next day, I came out, and I told him. And I told him what had happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Russians came to liberate?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came, they took me in the house. And later, when all of these survivors were in the town, he still kept me. It so happened my friend-- we went together to school. He survived. He came to the town. I didn't know for what reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was in the town. He saw-- his name was Yidli. He told him-- do you know whom I have? He says, me. So he told him, tell them next morning, he should come to this and this house in our town. And he will be over there with horses and wagon. He will take me where all the survivors got together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this way, he took me to Zdolbunow. Yeah, I want to mention here one thing. I wasn't married. I was the-- I was the whole one-- the whole who built everything-- who did everything. My father was a sick man. My father, during the-- during the First World War, they were from the front-- the towns, the Jewish people ran away. And they came to our town as refugees. And we took them-- I was a child. We took them in. And we gave them food. And we gave them clothing. And we kept them. In Russia, there was organization-- a Jewish organization [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like here. And they kept helping, giving clothing and money for the refugees for the towns. My father was very active. He went to Kiev, it's the capital of-- of Ukraine, to get help for the all the refugees that they were in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's from the first war.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the first war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he ate something. Anyway, he was sick, my father. He was sick, my father. So I didn't let him-- my father wants to give charity. So I didn't want him to come to me to ask for money. I did appreciate what my father did for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent me to a town 240 miles from our town to the-- to the high school, he sent me. I did appreciate it. And I remember what he did. And I try to pay him back, to let him-- to let him see how much I do appreciate what he did for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sent him to [NON-ENGLISH], I send him-- and I-- and I let him-- he was the cashier. He kept the money. He wanted to give charity-- he wanted-- he didn't have to come to me to ask for it. I let him do it-- to be able.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came where all the Jews reunited-- all the survivors were reunited after the liberators?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my friend came and he took me over there, yes. I was single. When I-- when I came-- when I came over there, I found my wife with  my older daughter. My wife-- this was my friend, and we were neighbors, one house next to the order. And he was my best friend, my-- the previous husband. He was my best friend. He sent her away to a Polack. And he paid him he should keep her with the-- the daughter was, at that time, four years old. We felt already that eventually, they going to kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know, a few days or a week before they killed the whole town, he sent her away. But I wouldn't go through what she went through with the child. And how he-- how she saved-- I'm not going to tell her story. She was with that child four years old. And she-- and-- and she-- and she-- how did she save herself? About 16 months or more-- how did she save it? It's a lot. So when I came over there, I met her. She was with the child. The child was in that time six years old. So we got together, and I married her. And I-- I didn't adopt officially, just my older daughter. [CRYING] I love her like-- I love her. She is-- she is my child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- [SOBBING] I did remember, when she was two and three, I used to, once in a while, when I came to my friend, to the house, because the next house was next to us. I used to play with her. She was a beautiful little girl. She is still as she was. Then she's-- [CRYING] when she was-- she is-- she is beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I married-- I married my wife. And-- What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is-- is Nuna. They call her Nuna. So-- so I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her daughter-- and your daughter, her daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Claire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Claire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you call her at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She calls me Dad today, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no-- she had a-- Claire was her name at that time, too? From the child? The child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her Jewish name is Haya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Haya.

speaker: interviewee
text: Haya, the Jewish name. So she-- she-- I don't know if she-- she doesn't-- she doesn't remember her father-- not at all. She knows-- knows no other-- only father, I am. And I know she's my-- [CRYING] she's my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Nuna's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a name-- what the-- what the-- there is such a Jewish name. They called her in Jewish, Nuna. Nuna and-- Nuna is-- we came here, she became Ann, A-N-N, she became. She came A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a family-- the three of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I married her, and we got a job. And it so happened over there, there was a Ukrainian in this town-- there was over there a Ukrainian, what I used to-- [LAUGHS] I used to give a lot of money, did a lot for me with the railroad cars. You see, he was the head of the conductors. So I-- I had to-- I had to give him money, because he did a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, when I came to this town, he was from this town. And he had a big house. So he took-- he took me into his house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me half of the house. And I lived in this-- and I lived in this-- in-- in this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your wife and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were any schools for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were. My older daughter-- she went in the beginning-- she went to a Russian school. Now, she does [NON-ENGLISH]. My older daughter is-- she went over there, she did. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She's very-- she's-- both my-- both my daughters are very bright.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your daughter? When was your second daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second daughter was born already. You see, I was working for the-- for the Russians-- I mean, for the Soviet Union-- till 1946. Then they came-- then they passed a law-- only the Jewish people who were citizens of the-- of Poland had the right to go to Poland, because they were over there anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided, it's no-- I don't want to stay in the town. There were no Jews. There was nobody. So I decided I will go to Poland. So I went-- I went-- the head-- the head-- where, I mean-- the-- they call them a supervisor, is not the head of this office, in the railroad-- he was a general. Because of the railroad, they didn't take him to the army. Because-- So I went to him, and I told him a lie. I told him somebody told me, my-- my sister saved herself. And she's in Poland. So I want to go and find her. So he gave-- so he gave me a railroad car. And I went to Poland. With your wife and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and my child. And later on came-- came off a guy who ran away to the Soviet Union during the war. I took him in, also. And he went with me. I took him in, into my apartment. And he lived with me. And he to Poland with me. And in Poland, while-- while we were living in Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went straight to Lodz from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because over there, they were-- her cousin was already-- what she-- what she saved herself, we took-- well, so she was-- she was over there, so we went over there. And over there, and over there, I met a doctor from our town who was a communist. And he ran away to the Soviet Union. And he was in the army as a doctor. I met him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Lodz. I met him over there. And I didn't want to stay in-- I don't know how long we were in Poland. We were perhaps half a year-- I don't know. And yeah, I guess still '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz, you stayed with her cousin-- your wife's cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she took us out. She had over there an apartment. She took us out. And we stayed over there. And when he-- when I met this doctor, I asked him, and he-- and he brought his friends, a colonel. And he took us, and-- and with my-- with my friend, and he-- and he took us by-- with a truck. And he brought us to Berlin. In Berlin, they were three zones-- English zone, American, and the-- and the Soviet Union. And over there was the first DP camp. They called it Schlactensse.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-L-A-C-H-T-E-N-S-S-E. And over there was the first DP camp where the Joint also was over there active.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
